content: Messenger RNA (mRNA) functions as a critical intermediary in the flow of genetic information from DNA to protein synthesis. Synthesized in the nucleus via transcription, mRNA carries a complementary nucleotide sequence derived from the DNA template strand, encoding the precise amino acid sequence of a polypeptide. Once processed—capped, spliced, and polyadenylated—mRNA translocates to the cytoplasm, where ribosomes engage it. The ribosome reads the mRNA codons sequentially, matching each triplet to its cognate tRNA anticodon, thus directing the stepwise polymerization of amino acids into a nascent polypeptide chain. Importantly, the mRNA not only encodes primary structure but also modulates translation efficiency and localization through untranslated regions (UTRs) and regulatory elements, facilitating dynamic cellular responses. mRNA’s transient nature and susceptibility to degradation underpin tightly controlled protein expression, enabling rapid adaptation to intracellular and extracellular signals. Therefore, mRNA acts as a finely tuned molecular courier, translating static genomic information into functional proteomic outputs.
